topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unforgiving white against the muted backdrop of my cluttered desk. Dr. Alistair’s departure was confirmed. I stared at the email, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, sinking feeling settling deep in my chest. For weeks, his presence had been the anchor, the steady current guiding my research ship. Now, the anchor was being hauled up, leaving me adrift in choppy, uncertain waters. I slowly pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding unnaturally loud in the silence of the cramped office. My hands felt strangely heavy, the ink on my fingers seeming to resist the simple act of gripping a pen. I just wanted to curl up under my duvet and pretend the world hadn't tilted on its axis.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I managed, the sound of my own voice thin and reedy. I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting him to materialize, to offer some reassuring nod, some familiar, gravelly encouragement. But the silence remained, thick and suffocating. My gaze drifted to the stack of annotated manuscripts, each page suddenly feeling like a monument to a path that was about to vanish. I rubbed my temples, the pressure behind my eyes building into a dull ache. I picked up my coffee mug, but the warmth did nothing to penetrate the pervasive chill that had settled over my entire frame. Every movement felt labored, as if moving through water. I leaned my forehead against the cool surface of the desk, letting my breathing become shallow and uneven.

[story 3]

The announcement hit me like a physical blow. I didn't move for a long moment, just sat there, frozen in my ergonomic chair. My eyes tracked the text on the screen, unable to process the finality of it. A strange numbness began to creep up my arms, a slow withdrawal of energy that made even the act of blinking feel like a monumental effort. I looked around the lab, at the half-finished experiments and the familiar clutter, and everything seemed muted, washed out. I finally pushed myself up, the motion slow and deliberate, like a machine struggling to engage. My gait was unsteady, each step a conscious negotiation with gravity. I needed to find a place to sit down, anywhere, just to stop fighting the urge to simply collapse into the nearest available surface.
